coredata.dc:title: Optical thermometry based on ZnAl2O4:Eu3+ with carbon dots incorporation
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Su-Hua
Liao, Shun-Ming
Tsai, Yi-Yan
Wang, Chia-Ho
Ho, Chih-Chieh
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, red carbon dot-incorporated ZnAl2O4:Eu3+ (CDs@ZAO:Eu3+) nanophosphors were synthesized using the sol-gel method, and CDs were prepared using green approaches with leaves and synthetic chemicals as sources. The incorporation of CDs in ZAO:Eu3+ reduced the positive and negative defects in the phosphor, resulting in improved crystallinity. A large spectral overlap between the emission of CDs and the absorption of ZAO:Eu3+ was observed. A 15.4% enhancement in the luminescence intensity (I) was analyzed from CDs@ZAO:Eu3+. The energy transfer process between CDs and Eu3+ was demonstrated. The temperature (T) and I had a linear relationship that could be fitted to a function of
T = 1276.5 − 970.9 I
. The absolute sensitivity and relative sensitivity were 1.03 × 10−3 K−1 and 1.12 × 10−3 K−1, respectively, at 383 K. Thus, CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ is a promising material for non-contact optical thermometry applications.

Energy transfer
Optical thermometry

head.ce:title: Optical thermometry based on ZnAl2O4:Eu3+ with carbon dots incorporation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Engineering, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology, Kaohsiung 807, Taiwan, ROC

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronic Engineering, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electronic Engineering, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology, Kaohsiung 807, Taiwan, R.O.C.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, red carbon dot-incorporated ZnAl2O4:Eu3+ (CDs@ZAO:Eu3+) nanophosphors were synthesized using the sol-gel method, and CDs were prepared using green approaches with leaves and synthetic chemicals as sources. The incorporation of CDs in ZAO:Eu3+ reduced the positive and negative defects in the phosphor, resulting in improved crystallinity. A large spectral overlap between the emission of CDs and the absorption of ZAO:Eu3+ was observed. A 15.4% enhancement in the luminescence intensity (I) was analyzed from CDs@ZAO:Eu3+. The energy transfer process between CDs and Eu3+ was demonstrated. The temperature (T) and I had a linear relationship that could be fitted to a function of
T = 1276.5 − 970.9 I
. The absolute sensitivity and relative sensitivity were 1.03 × 10−3 K−1 and 1.12 × 10−3 K−1, respectively, at 383 K. Thus, CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ is a promising material for non-contact optical thermometry applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphors are luminescent materials and are used as illuminants in fluorescent lamps, field emission displays, and light-emitting diodes [1–3]. Apart from illuminants, they have applications in various fields: photocatalysts [3–6], solar cells [7–9], biosensors [7,10–13], anti-counterfeiting [14,15], optical data storages [16,17], and latent fingerprint recognitions [18–21].

Phosphor-based thermometry has emerged as a useful non-contact optical temperature sensor [22–26]. The temperature can be measured by analyzing changes in the optical parameters of phosphors: the fluorescence intensity [27–29], bandwidth [30], bandshift [31], and lifetime [32–34]. A relative sensitivity (S r) of 0.32%K−1 was achieved using YOF:Ho3+/Yb3+ as a thermal sensor analyzed by the fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) with efficient near-infrared (NIR) to visible upconversion [27]. Y2O3:Er3+ thermometry operating within the 1st and 2nd biological window exhibited a high S r of 2.02 ± 0.06%K−1 at 293 K as analyzed using the FIR. [28]. Thermometry based on the temperature effect of the emission bandwidth is associated with the thermal broadening of vibrational energy levels and the increase in the number of thermally activated vibrational levels. The S r of [L3Cu2](BF4)2 had a value of 0.11%K−1 at 298 K calculated using the bandwidth thermo-sensing technique [30]. The doping of Mg2+ in BaLaCa1−x Mg x SbO6 resulted in the efficient improvement of absolute sensitivity (S a) from 1.22 μsK−1 to 4.88 μsK−1 in lifetime-based luminescence thermometry [33].

The Eu3+-doped alkaline earth aluminates MAl2O4 (M = Ca, Sr, Ba, Zn), such as ZnAl2O4:Eu3+ (ZAO:Eu3+) [1,2,4,20,21] have attracted considerable attention for luminescent materials used in optical thermometry because of their high physical and chemical stability and efficient bio-window band emissions [35,36]. However, the luminescence of MAl2O4-based phosphors must be further improved to achieve the best performance. Commonly used methods include synthesis parameter optimization [37–42], core-shell structure configuration [41], surface modification [43], and flux addition [44]. Additionally, charge compensation [45], sensitizer incorporation [46,47], and energy transfer approaches [48] are also preferred.

Recently, new strategies have been developed to enhance the luminescence of the phosphors. Growing a textured layer of crystalline anisotropic SrAl2O4:Eu,Dy on sapphire doubled its phosphorescence efficiency [49]. Additionally, incorporating carbon dots (CDs) into phosphors effectively improved their luminous efficiency [50–52]. Blue CDs coating provided a blue emitting component which improved the colorimetric properties of Dy3+-doped tellurite and silicate glasses [51]. CDs@ZnGa2O4 composites exhibited significantly enhanced photoluminescence (PL) intensity because of the increased disorder and vacancy concentrations [53]. The effect of CD incorporation on the improvement of phosphor luminescence was also investigated via the energy transfer mechanism. Energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ significantly enhanced the luminescence of multicolor YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites [54]. Furthermore, ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composites exhibited enhanced luminescence, which was attributed to the energy transfer between N-doped CDs (NCDs) and Tm3+ ions [55]. It was also reported that CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ had a higher PL intensity than the YBO3:Eu3+ attributing to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism between CDs and Eu3+ [56].

CDs have been added to MAl2O4-based phosphors of ZnAl2O4 (ZAO) to achieve specific luminescence features. Hollow ZAO:Eu3+ nanophosphors with high surface areas and excellent red emission were prepared using CDs as hard templates [57]. Embedded CDs enabled ZAO nanoparticles to achieve green/yellow and orange/red phosphorescence [58,59]. The multi-emissive phosphorescence of CDs@ZAO was attributed to the protection of CD triplet emission by the ZAO matrix through the confinement effect, spin-orbit coupling mechanism, and efficient energy transfer from inorganic trap states to CD triplet states [60].

Although the properties of CDs@ZAO have been reported, the effect of CD incorporation on the luminescence of ZAO:Eu3+ has not been investigated. Hence, in this study, CDs were incorporated into ZAO:Eu3+ to improve the PL intensity of phosphors. The crystallinity, surface morphology, PL properties, and thermal stability of ZAO:Eu3+ phosphors with and without CD incorporation were analyzed. Additionally, the optical thermometry characteristics of the CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ phosphor are discussed.

ZAO:Eu3+ red phosphors were synthesized using the sol-gel method. Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (99%, Alfa Aesar), Al(NO3)3·9 H2O (98%, SHOWA), and Eu(CH3COO)3 (99.9%, Alfa Aesar) were selected as raw materials. The materials were weighed according to their stoichiometric ratios, added to deionized (DI) water, and stirred vigorously for 1 h. Citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5%, SHOWA) was added to the solution during stirring, and the ratio of Zn(NO3)2·6 H2O to citric acid was maintained at 1:0.75. After the completion of the reaction, the mixture was dried at 120 °C for 24 h. The obtained powder was ground for 10 min and then annealed at 600–900 °C in air for 4–8 h.

Two different chemical reactions were used to prepare CDs. The reaction using yongshuh leaves as the raw source and heat as the reactant was based on green synthesis and thermal decomposition. First, the leaves were washed and dried in an oven, crushed, and subsequently heated at 280 °C for 4 h. The obtained carbon powder was added to DI water, milled with Zr balls for 100 min, and centrifuged three times at 6000 rpm for 30 min each. Subsequently, a pale-yellow CD solution was obtained (Fig. S1), and the CDs were named leaf-CDs (L-CDs).

CDs were also prepared using anhydrous citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5%, SHOWA) and hexamethylenetetramine (HMT, C6H12N4, 99%, Alfa Aesar) as raw sources. 3 g of citric acid and 6 g of HMT were mixed in 20 ml DI water, stirred vigorously for 1 h, and heated in an oven at 120 °C for 24 h. The mixed solution was initially transparent but turned into brown-black solids upon heating. This indicates that carbon powder was produced, which was subsequently ground, added to DI water, and centrifuged three times at 6000 rpm for 30 min each to obtain a CD solution. The obtained CDs were named HMT-CDs (H-CDs).
In addition, for the preparation of CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ phosphor, the synthesis process was the same as that of ZAO:Eu3+ except that the CDs were added to the solution while stirring.

The crystallinity, morphology, and composition of phosphors were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8, USA), field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6330 TF), and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), respectively. The chemical states of elements were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; JEOL JAMP-9500 F). The energy absorption and emission characteristics were analyzed from PL and PL excitation (PLE) spectra using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000). The excitation (
λ
ex
) and emission (
λ
em
) wavelengths were set at 394 nm and 616 nm, respectively.

The reported structure of ZAO indicates that ZAO is crystallized in a cubic structure (space group

) and has close-packed arrays of oxygen atoms [61–68]. Zn2+ and Al3+ cations occupy tetrahedral and octahedral coordinating sites, respectively [69–72]. When Eu3+ ion is doped into the ZAO lattice, Zn2+ (ionic radius r = 0.060 nm) [73,74] is substituted by Eu3+ (r = 0.095 nm) [75,76]. This substitution must be charge-compensated by the defects of positive/negative charges, which induces defect-association interactions. is a cluster of three defects. Crystalline analysis showed the slight shifting of XRD peaks to lower diffraction angles due to the substitution of Zn2+ by Eu3+ (Fig. S2, Fig. 1(a)–1(d)). However, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the (311) peak increased from 0.441° to 0.520°, 0.582°, and 0.647° when the Eu3+ concentration increased from 13 to 15, 17, and 19 mol%, respectively. This indicated that Eu3+ doping declined the crystallinity of ZAO. The average sizes of ZAO:Eu3+ nanocrystallites derived from the (311) peak using the Scherrer equation were approximately 19.9, 16.8, 15.1, and 13.5 nm as the Eu3+ concentration increased from 13 to 15, 17, and 19 mol%, respectively. The size calculated from the XRD pattern is related to the nanocrystallites aggregated within a particle [79]; thus, it is different from the value obtained via SEM (Fig. S3). Optimal crystallinity and luminescence were obtained when ZAO:Eu3+ was doped with 17 mol% Eu3+ and synthesized at 700 °C for 7 h (Fig. S4).

The energy absorption and emission characteristics of ZAO:Eu3+ are shown in Fig. 2(a). The PLE spectrum shows a broad

charge transfer band with a peak at 299 nm. PLE peaks at 362, 382, 394, 413, 464, and 531 nm are attributed to electron transitions in Eu3+ from 7F0 to 5D4, 5L7, 5L6, 5D3, 5D2, and 5D1 energy levels, respectively [80–82]. Additionally, PL peaks at 577, 592, 616, 654, and 705 nm are ascribed to 5D0 → 7F0–4 electron transitions in Eu3+.

The PL spectra of ZAO:Eu3+ analyzed in the temperature range of 30–110 °C are shown in Fig. 2(b). Changes in the temperature did not alter the emission color of the phosphor. is shown in Fig. 2(c). The ZAO:Eu3+ has a high E a of 0.29 eV, which makes it physically and chemically stable for optical thermometry applications.

The thermal stability of ZAO:Eu3+ was further improved by the addition of CDs. The improvement in the crystallinity and luminescence of CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ was also investigated. The XRD patterns in Fig. 1(e) and 1(f) demonstrate that no miscellaneous phases were developed when L- and H-CDs were incorporated into ZAO:Eu3+. No CD diffraction peaks were observed as the incorporation of CDs was minimum. The FWHM values of XRD (311) peaks were 0.5824°, 0.54958°, and 0.53469° when the ZAO:Eu3+ phosphors were prepared without CDs and with L-CDs and H-CDs, respectively. The decreased FWHM value indicates an improved crystallinity of CDs@ZAO:Eu3+. This was presumably due to the incorporation of CDs, which induced an oxidation/reduction effect and reduced positive and negative defects in the phosphor. The luminescence of CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ phosphors depended on their crystallization, particle morphologies, and composition stoichiometry. Fig. 3 shows the morphologies of CDs. Pentagonal L-CDs with large diameters of approximately 1–2 µm were synthesized when dried leaves were not smashed before baking (Fig. 3(a)). In contrast, nanoparticles of L-CDs with diameters less than 100 nm were synthesized when dried leaves were ground before baking (Fig. 3(b)). SEM images also showed that H-CDs prepared with synthetic chemicals had particle sizes of approximately 80–130 nm, close to those of L-CDs and ZAO:Eu3+ (Fig. 3(c)).

Fig. 4 shows the SEM images (Fig. 4(a) and 4(d)) and ion mappings ((Fig. 4(b) and 4(e)) of the CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ phosphors. Ion mapping images confirmed that CDs were homogeneously blended in the phosphors (Figs. S5 and S6). The carbon composition incorporated in the phosphors was determined via EDS analysis (Fig. 4(c) and 4(f)), wherein the EDS peak at approximately 1.74 keV was referred to the Si pedestal. The elemental composition ratio of Zn:Al:O in CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ was close to the stoichiometric ratio of 1:2:4 ( Table 1). The concentration of carbon in CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ was 19–22 at%. XPS analysis shows that a C 1 s peak at 288 eV was examined (Fig. S7), which was attributed to O-CO binding on the phosphor surface. This confirmed the incorporation of CDs in ZAO:Eu3+.

The PLE and PL spectra of the L-CDs and H-CDs were analyzed to investigate the energy absorption and emission characteristics of CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ ( Fig. 5(a) and 5(b)). The L-CDs exhibited a broad PLE spectrum with a peak at 397 nm, which was close to the PLE peak of Eu3+ at 394 nm (Fig. 2(a)). In contrast, a narrower PLE peak at 468 nm was observed for H-CDs. The PLE peaks of L- and H-CDs were attributed to electron transitions from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO, π) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO, π * ) energy level in CDs [84–86].

For the luminescence of CDs, the
π
*
→ π
transition in the core states contributed to blue emission. However, the presence of surface states narrowed the energy levels, and the corresponding
π
*
→ π
electron transitions led to broadband blue-green emission. The combination of core and surface state emissions resulted in an asymmetric PL spectral pattern. PL peaks were observed at 490 and 540 nm for L-CD and H-CD, respectively. These broad PL spectra are beneficial for achieving a large spectral overlap between the PL of CDs and the PLE of phosphors for energy transfer [75,87,88], as shown in Fig. 5(c) and 5(d).

The L-CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ (Fig. 5(c)) had a greater spectral overlap than that of H-CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ (Fig. 5(d)). Hence, the excitation energy can be transferred more efficiently from L-CDs to ZAO:Eu3+. The excitation energy (
λ
ex
= 394 nm) was well absorbed by L-CDs, then being transferred to Eu3+ via the Förster or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism and released by radiative emission. Consequently, the PL intensity of L-CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ was 15.4% higher than that of ZAO:Eu3+ ( Fig. 6).

The energy transfer processes between the CDs and Eu3+ activator are shown in Fig. 7 [89]. When CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ was excited by photons with a wavelength of 394 nm, electron transitions occurred in Eu3+ from the 7F0 to 5L6 energy level then to 5D0 through non-radiative relaxation and finally to 7F0–4 to yield red emission. Meanwhile, electrons in CDs absorbed photon energy and transferred from HOMO to LUMO. The excited electrons were then relaxed to the energy levels of core and surface states through non-radiative transition and finally transferred to the 5D0–2 energy levels of Eu3+ via the FRET mechanism.

The CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ phosphors have stable physical and chemical properties and possess high luminescence efficiency, making them suitable for multifunctional applications, such as optical thermometry. The temperature dependence of the phosphor luminescence can be analyzed using the fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) [90–100], lifetime alternation [101–103], and spectrum phase shift [103,104]. In this study, the FIR method, whose value is linearly proportional to thermal quenching with an increase in temperature, was chosen for analyzing the optical thermometry of CDs@ZAO:Eu3+. The FIR value (I) at a wavelength of 616 nm was calculated from the ratio of PL intensity at temperature (T) to the PL intensity at 303 K. The I-T plots of the ZAO:Eu3+ phosphors with and without the incorporation of L-CDs are shown in Fig. 8. Phosphors were heated at 303–383 K and wavelengths used were
λ
ex
= 394 nm and
λ
em
= 616 nm. The incorporation of CDs improved the thermal stability of the phosphor. The FIR value is thermally quenched linearly with increasing temperature, which can be fitted to linear functions of
T = 796.5 − 492.6 I
and
T = 1276.5 − 970.9 I
when the ZAO:Eu3+ was prepared without and with the L-CDs incorporation, respectively. The absolute sensitivity S a is defined as
∂ I
∂ T
, and the relative sensitivity S r as
1
I
×
∂ I
∂ T
[97–100]. The calculated S a was 1.03 × 10−3 K−1 and S r was 1.12 × 10−3 K−1 at 383 K. Table 2 lists the characteristics of MAl2O4 (M = Mg, Sr, Zn) based thermometry [104–111]. It shows that the thermometric sensitivity of the CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ phosphor is comparable to that of previously reported studies. The CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ phosphor exhibits high thermal stability, which increases its potential for high-temperature sensing applications.

ZAO:Eu3+ was synthesized using the sol-gel method. The substitution of Eu3+ to the Zn2+ induced defect association interactions and declined the crystallinity of the phosphors. The ZAO:Eu3+ phosphor had a high E a of 0.29 eV, making it physically and chemically stable for optical thermometry applications.

CDs were prepared via green synthesis. A better crystallinity of CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ was obtained because of the decrease in the number of positive and negative defects. L-CDs@ZAO:Eu3+ achieved a 15.4% enhancement in the PL intensity. The energy transfer processes between CDs and Eu3+ were illustrated. The temperature dependence of the phosphor luminescence was analyzed using the FIR method. The I-T relationship can be fitted to a linear function of
T = 1276.5 − 970.9 I
. The values of S a and S r were 1.03 × 10−3 K−1 and 1.12 × 10−3 K−1 at 383 K, respectively. This shows that CDs@ZnAl2O4:Eu3+ is a promising material for non-contact optical thermometry.

Su-Hua Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Shun-Ming Liao: Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yi-Yan Tsai: Investigation. Chia-Ho Wang: Investigation. Chih-Chieh Ho: Investigation.
